title: International Date Line

infobox.value: This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/International_Date_Line "Special:EditPage/International Date Line") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(July 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

(**IDL**) is the line extending between the [South](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") and [North Poles](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") that is the boundary between one calendar day and the next. It passes through the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), roughly following the [180.0°](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian") [line of longitude](/wiki/Line_of_longitude "Line of longitude") and deviating to pass around some territories and island groups. Crossing the date line eastbound decreases the date by one day, while crossing the date line westbound increases the date.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Line dividing one calendar day from the next

"Date line" redirects here. For other uses, see [Dateline (disambiguation)](/wiki/Dateline_(disambiguation) "Dateline (disambiguation)").

For the Ladytron song, see [International Dateline (song)](/wiki/International_Dateline_(song) "International Dateline (song)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/International_Date_Line.png/250px-International_Date_Line.png)](/wiki/File:International_Date_Line.png)

The International Date Line around the [antimeridian](/wiki/Antimeridian "Antimeridian") (180° longitude).

The **International Date Line** (**IDL**) is the line extending between the [South](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") and [North Poles](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") that is the boundary between one calendar day and the next. It passes through the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), roughly following the [180.0°](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian") [line of longitude](/wiki/Line_of_longitude "Line of longitude") and deviating to pass around some territories and island groups. Crossing the date line eastbound decreases the date by one day, while crossing the date line westbound increases the date.

The line is a [cartographic](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") convention and is not defined by international law. This has made it difficult for cartographers to agree on its precise course and has allowed countries through whose waters it passes to move it at times for their convenience.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/87/Dateline-animation-3deg-borderonly-180px.gif)](/wiki/File:Dateline-animation-3deg-borderonly-180px.gif)

A simplified illustration of the relation between the International Date Line, the date, and the time of day. Each color represents a different date.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/International_date_line.png/330px-International_date_line.png)](/wiki/File:International_date_line.png)

Example depicting situation at 04:00 [GMT](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time") Tuesday. (Times are approximate, since time zone boundaries generally do not exactly coincide with meridians. Night and day is illustrative only; daylight hours depend on latitude and time of year.)

### Circumnavigating the globe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Circumnavigating the globe")]

People traveling westward [around the world](/wiki/Circumnavigation "Circumnavigation") must set their clocks:

* Back by one hour for every 15° of longitude crossed, and
* Forward by 24 hours upon crossing the International Date Line.

People traveling eastward must set their clocks:

* Forward by one hour for every 15° of longitude crossed, and
* Back by 24 hours upon crossing the International Date Line.

Moving forward or back 24 hours generally also implies a one day date change.

### Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Description")]

This description is based on the most common understanding of the *de facto* International Date Line. See [§ De facto and de jure date lines](#De_facto_and_de_jure_date_lines) below, and map above at right.

The IDL is roughly based on the [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") of 180° longitude, roughly down the middle of the Pacific Ocean, and halfway around the world from the [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian"), the successor to the historic [Greenwich prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)") running through the [Royal Greenwich Observatory](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"). In many places, the IDL follows the 180° meridian exactly. In other places, however, the IDL deviates east or west away from that meridian. These various deviations generally accommodate the political and economic affiliations of the affected areas.

Proceeding from north to south, the first deviation of the IDL from 180° is to pass to the east of [Wrangel Island](/wiki/Wrangel_Island "Wrangel Island") and the [Chukchi Peninsula](/wiki/Chukchi_Peninsula "Chukchi Peninsula"), the easternmost part of Russian [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). (Wrangel Island lies directly on the meridian at 71°32′N 180°0′E, also noted as 71°32′N 180°0′W.)[[2]](#cite_note-wrengell-2) It then passes through the [Bering Strait](/wiki/Bering_Strait "Bering Strait") between the [Diomede Islands](/wiki/Diomede_Islands "Diomede Islands") at a distance of 1.5 kilometres (0.93 mi) from each island at 168°58′37″ W.[[3]](#cite_note-Russia-US_maritime_boundary-3) It then bends considerably west of 180°, passing west of [St. Lawrence Island](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Island "St. Lawrence Island") and [St. Matthew Island](/wiki/St._Matthew_Island "St. Matthew Island").

The IDL crosses between the U.S. [Aleutian Islands](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands "Aleutian Islands") ([Attu Island](/wiki/Attu_Island "Attu Island") being the westernmost) and the [Commander Islands](/wiki/Commander_Islands "Commander Islands"), which belong to Russia. It then bends southeast again to return to 180°. Thus, all of Russia is to the west of the IDL, and all of the United States is to the east except for the insular areas of [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands"), and [Wake Island](/wiki/Wake_Island "Wake Island"), reaching the hypothetical, but not used UTC–13:00 time zone.

The IDL remains on the 180° meridian until passing the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). Two U.S.-owned uninhabited atolls, [Howland Island](/wiki/Howland_Island "Howland Island") and [Baker Island](/wiki/Baker_Island "Baker Island"), just north of the equator in the central Pacific Ocean (and ships at sea between 172.5°W and 180°), have the *earliest*[[a]](#cite_note-5) time on Earth ([UTC−12:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9212:00 "UTC−12:00") hours).[[5]](#cite_note-timeNearIDL1-6)

The IDL circumscribes [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") by swinging far to the east, almost reaching the [150°W meridian](/wiki/150th_meridian_west "150th meridian west"). Kiribati's easternmost islands, the southern [Line Islands](/wiki/Line_Islands "Line Islands") south of [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), have the *latest*[[b]](#cite_note-7) time on Earth ([UTC+14:00](/wiki/UTC%2B14:00 "UTC+14:00") hours).[[5]](#cite_note-timeNearIDL1-6)

South of Kiribati, the IDL returns westward but remains east of 180°, passing between [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa") and [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa").[[6]](#cite_note-BorneoPost-8) Accordingly, Samoa, [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau"), [Wallis and Futuna](/wiki/Wallis_and_Futuna "Wallis and Futuna"), [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"), [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu"), and New Zealand's [Kermadec Islands](/wiki/Kermadec_Islands "Kermadec Islands") and [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") are all west of the IDL and have the same date. American Samoa, the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands"), [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue"), and [French Polynesia](/wiki/French_Polynesia "French Polynesia") are east of the IDL and one day behind.

The IDL then bends southwest to return to 180°. It follows that meridian until reaching [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), which has [multiple time zones](/wiki/Time_in_Antarctica "Time in Antarctica"). Conventionally, the IDL is not drawn into Antarctica on most maps. (See [§ Cartographic practice and convention](#Cartographic_practice_and_convention) below.)

### Facts dependent on the IDL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Facts dependent on the IDL")]

According to the clock, the first areas to experience a new day and a [New Year](/wiki/New_Year "New Year") are islands that use UTC+14:00. These include portions of the [Republic of Kiribati](/wiki/Republic_of_Kiribati "Republic of Kiribati"), including [Millennium Island](/wiki/Millennium_Island "Millennium Island") and [Kiritimati](/wiki/Kiritimati "Kiritimati") in the [Line Islands](/wiki/Line_Islands "Line Islands"). The first major cities to experience a new day are [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland") and [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington"), New Zealand ([UTC+12:00](/wiki/UTC%2B12:00 "UTC+12:00") or [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") during daylight saving [time](/wiki/Time_in_New_Zealand "Time in New Zealand")).[[5]](#cite_note-timeNearIDL1-6)

A 1994 realignment of the IDL made Caroline Island one of the first points of land on Earth to reach January 1, 2000, on the calendar (UTC+14:00). As a result, this [atoll](/wiki/Atoll "Atoll") was renamed Millennium Island.[[7]](#cite_note-Millennium-9)

Every day for 2 hours from 10:00 to 12:00 UTC there are 3 different days on earth. Example: On Tuesday 10:33 UTC it is Monday 22:33 on [Baker Island](/wiki/Baker_Island "Baker Island") (US), 23:33 on [Midway](/wiki/Midway_Atoll "Midway Atoll") (US), [Pago Pago](/wiki/Pago_Pago,_American_Samoa "Pago Pago, American Samoa") (American Samoa) and [Alofi](/wiki/Alofi "Alofi") (Niue), Tuesday almost everywhere else on earth and Wednesday 00:33 in [Kiritimati](/wiki/Kiritimati "Kiritimati") (Kiribati) in the Line Islands. Then 1 hour 11 minutes later at 11:44 UTC it is Monday 23:44 on Baker Island, Tuesday almost everywhere else on earth, Wednesday 01:44 in Kiritimati and 00:44 in [Canton Island](/wiki/Canton_Island "Canton Island") (Kiribati) in the [Phoenix Islands](/wiki/Phoenix_Islands "Phoenix Islands"), [Apia](/wiki/Apia "Apia") (Samoa), [Atafu](/wiki/Atafu "Atafu") (Tokelau) and [Nukuʻalofa](/wiki/Nuku%CA%BBalofa "Nukuʻalofa") (Tonga) (also in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland") during summer when [NZDT](/wiki/Time_in_New_Zealand "Time in New Zealand") is observed). [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") (NZ) are also nominally 2 days ahead of Baker Island for 45 minutes in the winter ([CHAST](/wiki/Chatham_Standard_Time_Zone "Chatham Standard Time Zone")) and 1 hour 45 minutes in the summer (CHADT).[[5]](#cite_note-timeNearIDL1-6)

The areas that are the first to see the daylight of a new day vary by the season. Around the [June solstice](/wiki/June_solstice "June solstice"), the first area would be any place within the [Kamchatka Time Zone](/wiki/Kamchatka_Time "Kamchatka Time") (UTC+12:00) that is far enough north to experience [midnight sun](/wiki/Midnight_sun "Midnight sun") on the given date. At the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox"), the first place to see daylight would be the uninhabited Millennium Island in Kiribati, which is the easternmost land located west of the IDL.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Near the [December solstice](/wiki/December_solstice "December solstice"), the first places would be [Antarctic research stations](/wiki/Research_stations_in_Antarctica "Research stations in Antarctica") using [New Zealand Time](/wiki/Time_in_New_Zealand "Time in New Zealand") (UTC+13:00) during summer that experience midnight sun. These include [Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station](/wiki/Amundsen-Scott_South_Pole_Station "Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station"), [McMurdo Station](/wiki/McMurdo_Station "McMurdo Station"), [Scott Base](/wiki/Scott_Base "Scott Base") and [Zucchelli Station](/wiki/Zucchelli_Station "Zucchelli Station").[[5]](#cite_note-timeNearIDL1-6)[[8]](#cite_note-10)

## *De facto* and *de jure* date lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: De facto and de jure date lines")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/International_Date_Line "Special:EditPage/International Date Line") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(March 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

There are two ways [time zones](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") and thereby the location of the International Date Line are determined: one on land and adjacent [territorial waters](/wiki/Territorial_waters "Territorial waters"), and the other on open seas.

All nations unilaterally determine their standard [time zones](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone"), applicable only on land and adjacent territorial waters. This date line can be called *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* since it is not based on international law, but on national laws. These national zones do not extend into international waters.

The [nautical date line](/wiki/Nautical_date_line "Nautical date line"), not the same as the IDL, is a *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* construction determined by international agreement. It is the result of the 1917 [Anglo-French Conference on Time-keeping at Sea](/wiki/Anglo-French_Conference_on_Time-keeping_at_Sea "Anglo-French Conference on Time-keeping at Sea"), which recommended that all ships, both military and civilian, adopt hourly standard time zones on the high seas. The United States adopted its recommendation for U.S. military and merchant marine ships in 1920. This date line is implied but not explicitly drawn on time zone maps. It follows the [180° meridian](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian") except where it is interrupted by territorial waters adjacent to land, forming gaps—it is a pole-to-pole dashed line. The 15° [gore](/wiki/Gore_(segment) "Gore (segment)") that is offset from UTC by 12 hours is bisected by the nautical date line into two 7.5° gores that differ from UTC by ±12 hours.

In theory, ships are supposed to adopt the standard time of a country if they are within its territorial waters within 12 nautical miles (14 mi; 22 km) of land, then revert to international time zones (15° wide pole-to-pole [gores](/wiki/Gore_(segment) "Gore (segment)")) as soon as they leave. In practice, ships use these time zones only for radio communication and similar purposes. For internal (within-ship) purposes, such as work and meal hours, ships use a time zone of their own choosing.

### Cartographic practice and convention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cartographic practice and convention")]

The IDL on the map in this article and all other maps is based on the *de facto* line and is an artificial construct of cartographers, as the precise course of the line in international waters is arbitrary. The IDL does not extend into Antarctica on the world time zone maps by the United States [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA)[[9]](#cite_note-CIA-11) or the United Kingdom's [His Majesty's Nautical Almanac Office](/wiki/HM_Nautical_Almanac_Office "HM Nautical Almanac Office") (HMNAO).[[10]](#cite_note-HMNAO-12) The IDL on modern CIA maps now reflects the most recent shifts in the IDL.[[9]](#cite_note-CIA-11) (see: [§ Historic alterations](#Historic_alterations)) The current HMNAO map does not draw the IDL in conformity with recent shifts in the IDL; it draws a line virtually identical to that adopted by the UK's Hydrographic Office about 1900.[[11]](#cite_note-13) Instead, HMNAO labels island groups with their time zones, which do reflect the most recent IDL shifts.[[10]](#cite_note-HMNAO-12) This approach is consistent with the principle of national and nautical time zones: the islands of eastern Kiribati are actually "islands" of Asian date (west side of IDL) in a sea of American date (east side of IDL). Similarly, the western Aleutian Islands are islands of American date in a sea of Asian date.

No international organization, nor any treaty between nations, has fixed the IDL drawn by cartographers: the 1884 [International Meridian Conference](/wiki/International_Meridian_Conference "International Meridian Conference") explicitly refused to propose or agree to any time zones, stating that they were outside its purview. The conference resolved that the Universal Day, midnight-to-midnight [Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time") (now redefined and updated as [Coordinated Universal Time, or UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")), which it did agree to, "shall not interfere with the use of local or standard time where desirable".[[12]](#cite_note-imc-14) From this comes the utility and importance of UTC or "Z" ("Zulu") time: it permits a single universal reference for time that is valid for all points on the globe at the same moment.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

The 14th-century Arab geographer [Abulfeda](/wiki/Abulfeda "Abulfeda") predicted that circumnavigators would accumulate a one-day offset to the local date.[[13]](#cite_note-15) This phenomenon was confirmed in 1522 at the end of the [Magellan–Elcano expedition](/wiki/Magellan%27s_circumnavigation "Magellan's circumnavigation"), the first successful circumnavigation. After sailing westward around the world from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), the expedition called at [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde") for provisions on Wednesday, 9 July 1522 (ship's time). However, the locals told them that it was actually Thursday, 10 July 1522. The crew was surprised, as they had recorded each day of the three-year journey without omission.[[14]](#cite_note-16) Cardinal [Gasparo Contarini](/wiki/Gasparo_Contarini "Gasparo Contarini"), the Venetian ambassador to Spain, was the first European to give a correct explanation of the discrepancy.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

### Historic alterations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Historic alterations")]

#### Philippines (1521 and 1844)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Philippines (1521 and 1844)")]

Main article: [Philippine Standard Time](/wiki/Philippine_Standard_Time "Philippine Standard Time")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Datumsgrenze_MK1888.png/250px-Datumsgrenze_MK1888.png)](/wiki/File:Datumsgrenze_MK1888.png)

Erroneous International Date Line from the 1888 Encyclopædia the *[Meyers Konversations-Lexikon](/wiki/Meyers_Konversations-Lexikon "Meyers Konversations-Lexikon")*, running between the Spanish [Philippine Islands](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(1565%E2%80%931898) "History of the Philippines (1565–1898)") and British [Hong Kong](/wiki/History_of_Hong_Kong_(1800s%E2%80%931930s) "History of Hong Kong (1800s–1930s)"). The Philippine Islands and the rest of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") are shown on the eastern side of the IDL, even though they were moved to the western side in 1845. Also placed to the east of the IDL were the [Bonin Islands](/wiki/Bonin_Islands "Bonin Islands") and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), which are actually to the west of the line.

[Ferdinand Magellan](/wiki/Ferdinand_Magellan "Ferdinand Magellan") claimed the Philippines for Spain on Saturday, 16 March 1521, having sailed westward from [Seville](/wiki/Seville "Seville") across the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). As part of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") had its most important communication with [Acapulco](/wiki/Acapulco "Acapulco") in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), so it was on the eastern side of the IDL despite being on the western edge of the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). As a result, the Philippines was one day behind its [Asian](/wiki/Asia "Asia") neighbours for 323 years, 9 months and 2 days[[c]](#cite_note-18) from Saturday, 16 March 1521 *(Julian Calendar)* until Monday, 30 December 1844 *(Gregorian Calendar)*.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

After Mexico gained its independence from Spain on 27 September 1821, Philippine trade interests turned to [Imperial China](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") and adjacent areas, so the Philippines decided to join its Asian neighbours on the west side of the IDL.[[17]](#cite_note-20)[*[self-published source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] To advance the calendar by one day, on 16 August 1844 the then governor-general [Narciso Claveria](/wiki/Narciso_Claver%C3%ADa_y_Zald%C3%BAa "Narciso Clavería y Zaldúa"), ordered that Tuesday, 31 December 1844 should be removed from the calendar. Monday, 30 December 1844 was followed immediately by Wednesday, 1 January 1845. The change also applied to the other remaining Spanish colonies in the Pacific: the [Caroline Islands](/wiki/Caroline_Islands "Caroline Islands"), [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), [Mariana Islands](/wiki/Mariana_Islands "Mariana Islands"), [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands") and [Palau](/wiki/Palau "Palau") as part of the [Captaincy General of the Philippines](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_the_Philippines "Captaincy General of the Philippines").[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22) European publications were generally unaware of this change until the early 1890s, so they erroneously gave the International Date Line a large western bulge for the next half century.[[20]](#cite_note-23)

#### Tahiti & French Polynesia (early 1797 and late 1846)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tahiti & French Polynesia (early 1797 and late 1846)")]

On 5 March 1797, missionaries of the [London Missionary Society](/wiki/London_Missionary_Society "London Missionary Society") arrived on [Tahiti](/wiki/Tahiti "Tahiti") from England. They had first tried to pass [Cape Horn](/wiki/Cape_Horn "Cape Horn"), but failing that, went along [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope") and the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") instead. As such, they introduced the date of the eastern hemisphere on the island.[[21]](#cite_note-24) It was not until the ending of the [Franco-Tahitian War](/wiki/Franco-Tahitian_War "Franco-Tahitian War") and the restoration of the French [Protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") over the Tahitian Kingdom (which Tahitian nationalists had tried to fight off for two years of intense war with more than 1000 deaths) that the French commissioner [Armand Joseph Bruat](/wiki/Armand_Joseph_Bruat "Armand Joseph Bruat") and the regent of the Tahitian Kingdom Paraita ordered that Tahiti had to follow the western hemisphere on 29 December 1846.[[22]](#cite_note-25)[[23]](#cite_note-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)

#### Pitcairn Islands (1814)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pitcairn Islands (1814)")]

The International Date Line's history in relation to the Pitcairn Islands involves a miscalculation of Pitcairn's location by Captain [Philip Carteret](/wiki/Philip_Carteret "Philip Carteret") in 1767, which led [Fletcher Christian](/wiki/Fletcher_Christian "Fletcher Christian") to incorrectly search for the island, and the subsequent settlement of the island by mutineers from [HMS *Bounty*](/wiki/HMS_Bounty "HMS Bounty"), who initially used Asiatic dates (western side of the IDL or the Eastern Hemisphere date) before aligning with the American dates (east side of the IDL or the Western Hemisphere date).

In 1767, Captain Carteret of [HMS *Swallow*](/wiki/HMS_Swallow_(1745) "HMS Swallow (1745)") discovered Pitcairn Island, but miscalculated its location by 188 nautical miles (348 km; 216 mi) to the west. After the [mutiny on HMS *Bounty*](/wiki/Mutiny_on_the_Bounty "Mutiny on the Bounty") in 1789, Fletcher Christian and other mutineers, along with Tahitian and Polynesian crew members, settled on Pitcairn Island, seeking a remote and safe haven. Christian, relying on Carteret's inaccurate map, searched for the island in the wrong location, which contributed to the mutineers' decision to settle there.

The Pitcairn Islanders initially used Asiatic dates, but later switched to American dates, which aligned with their position east of the 180° meridian. The story of "Friday October" and "Thursday October" (names of mutineers) suggests that the Pitcairn Islanders initially used Asiatic dates, which were one day ahead of the American dates.

In 1814, British forces dealing with fugitive mutineers who fled to the Pitcairn Islands use dates that place the islands in the Western Hemisphere on the east side of the IDL. The Pitcairn Islanders eventually rectified their timekeeping and began to use American dates, which is the current practice.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Alaska (1867)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Alaska (1867)")]

See also: [Alaska Time Zone](/wiki/Alaska_Time_Zone "Alaska Time Zone") and [Time in Alaska](/wiki/Time_in_Alaska "Time in Alaska")

Alaska was on the western side of the International Date Line, since Russian settlers reached Alaska from [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). In addition, the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") was still using the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), which had fallen 12 days behind the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"). In 1867, the United States [purchased](/wiki/Alaska_Purchase "Alaska Purchase") [Russian America](/wiki/Russian_America "Russian America") and moved the territory to the east side of the International Date Line. The transfer ceremony took place at 3:30 p.m. local mean time (00:31 GMT) in the capital of New Archangel ([Sitka](/wiki/Sitka,_Alaska "Sitka, Alaska")), on Saturday, 7 October 1867 (Julian), which was Saturday, 19 October 1867 (Gregorian) in Europe. Since Alaska moved to the eastern side of the International Date Line, the date and time also moved back to 3:30 p.m. local time Friday, 18 October 1867 (00:31 GMT Saturday), now known as [Alaska Day](/wiki/Alaska_Day "Alaska Day").[[25]](#cite_note-transfer-28)[[26]](#cite_note-Sumner-29)

#### Samoan Islands and Tokelau (1892 and 2011)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Samoan Islands and Tokelau (1892 and 2011)")]

See also: [Samoa Time Zone](/wiki/Samoa_Time_Zone "Samoa Time Zone")

The [Samoan Islands](/wiki/Samoan_Islands "Samoan Islands"), now divided into [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa") and [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa"), were on the west side of the IDL until 1892. In that year, King [Mālietoa Laupepa](/wiki/Malietoa_Laupepa "Malietoa Laupepa") was persuaded by American traders to adopt the American date (three hours behind California) to replace the former Asian date (four hours ahead of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")). The change was made by repeating Monday, 4 July 1892, [American Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)").[[27]](#cite_note-30)

In 2011, Samoa shifted back to the west side of the IDL by removing Friday, 30 December 2011 from its calendar.[[28]](#cite_note-nypost-31) This changed the time zone from [UTC−11:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9211:00 "UTC−11:00") to [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") ([UTC-10](/wiki/UTC-10 "UTC-10") to [UTC+14](/wiki/UTC%2B14:00 "UTC+14:00") [DST](/wiki/DST "DST")).[[29]](#cite_note-HeraldSun-32) Samoa made the change because [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") have become its biggest trading partners, and also have large communities of expatriates. Being 21 hours behind made business difficult because having weekends on backward days meant only four days of the week were shared [workdays](/wiki/Workweek "Workweek").[[30]](#cite_note-33)

The IDL now passes between Samoa and American Samoa, which remains on the east (American) side of the line.[[31]](#cite_note-34)

[Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau") is a territory of New Zealand north of Samoa whose principal transportation and communications links with the rest of the world pass through [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa"). For that reason, Tokelau crossed the IDL along with Samoa in 2011, albeit strictly speaking 1 hour later, as they did not do Summer Time (Daylight Saving Time in American English), which Samoa did then.[[32]](#cite_note-Tokelau-35)

#### Cook Islands and Niue (1899)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Cook Islands and Niue (1899)")]

In 1899, the Cook Islands and Niue crossed the date line to the east side of the line upon becoming British protectorates.

#### Kwajalein (c. 1945 and 1993)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Kwajalein (c. 1945 and 1993)")]

[Kwajalein](/wiki/Kwajalein "Kwajalein") atoll, like the rest of the [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands"), passed from Spanish to German to Japanese control during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. During that period it was west of the IDL. Although Kwajalein formally became part of the [Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands](/wiki/Trust_Territory_of_the_Pacific_Islands "Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands") with the rest of the Marshalls after World War II, the United States established a military installation there. Because of that, Kwajalein used the Hawaiian date, so was effectively east of the International Date Line (unlike the rest of the Marshalls). Kwajalein returned to the west side of the IDL by removing Saturday, 21 August 1993 from its calendar. Moreover, Kwajalein's work week was changed to Tuesday through Saturday to match the Hawaiian work week of Monday through Friday on the other side of the IDL.[[33]](#cite_note-36)

#### Eastern Kiribati (1994)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Eastern Kiribati (1994)")]

Main article: [Time in Kiribati](/wiki/Time_in_Kiribati "Time in Kiribati")

As a British colony, the [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") colony was centered in the [Gilbert Islands](/wiki/Gilbert_Islands "Gilbert Islands"), just west of the IDL of the time. Upon independence in 1979, it acquired the claim to the [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_Islands "Phoenix Islands") and [Line Islands](/wiki/Line_Islands "Line Islands"), east of the IDL, from the United States. As a result, the country straddled the IDL. Government and commercial concerns on opposite sides of the line could only conduct routine business by radio or telephone on the four days of the week which were weekdays on both sides. To eliminate this anomaly, [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") introduced a change of date for its eastern half by removing Saturday, 31 December 1994 from its calendar. Because of this, Friday, 30 December 1994, was followed by Sunday, 1 January 1995. After the change, the IDL in effect moved eastward to go around the entire country. Strictly legal, the 1917 nautical IDL convention is still valid. For example, when it is Monday on Kiribati's islands, it is still Sunday in the surrounding ocean, though maps are usually not drawn this way.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

As a consequence of the 1994 change, Kiribati's easternmost territory, the [Line Islands](/wiki/Line_Islands "Line Islands"), including the inhabited island of [Kiritimati (Christmas Island)](/wiki/Kiritimati "Kiritimati"), started the year 2000 before any other country, a feature upon which the Kiribati government capitalized as a potential tourist draw.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Date lines according to religious principles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Date lines according to religious principles")]

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Christianity")]

Further information: [Seventh-day Adventist Church of Tonga § Date Line controversy](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church_of_Tonga#Date_Line_controversy "Seventh-day Adventist Church of Tonga")

Generally, the Christian calendar and Christian churches recognize the IDL. [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), for example, is celebrated on 25 December (according to either the [Gregorian](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") or the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), depending upon which of the two is used by the particular church) as that date falls in countries located on either side of the IDL. Thus, whether it is Western Christmas or Orthodox Christmas, Christians in Samoa, immediately west of the IDL, will celebrate the holiday a day before Christians in *American* Samoa, which is immediately *east* of the IDL.

A problem with the general rule above arises in certain Christian churches that solemnly observe a [Sabbath](/wiki/Sabbath "Sabbath") day as a particular day of the week, when those churches are located in countries near the IDL. Notwithstanding the difference in dates, the same sunrise happened over American Samoa as happens over Samoa a few minutes later, and the same sunset happens over Samoa as happened over American Samoa a few minutes earlier. In other words, the secular days are *legally* different but they are *physically* the same; that causes questions to arise under religious law. Because the IDL is an arbitrary imposition, the question can arise as to which Saturday on either side of the IDL (or, more fundamentally, on either side of 180 degree longitude) is the "real" Saturday. This issue (which also arises in [Judaism](#Judaism)) is a particular problem for [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventists "Seventh-day Adventists"), [Seventh Day Baptists](/wiki/Seventh_Day_Baptists "Seventh Day Baptists"), and similar churches located in countries near the IDL.

In [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"), [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church_of_Tonga "Seventh-day Adventist Church of Tonga") (who usually observe Saturday, the [seventh-day Sabbath](/wiki/Seventh-day_Sabbath "Seventh-day Sabbath")) observe Sunday because Tonga lies east of the 180° meridian. Sunday as observed in Tonga (west of the IDL, as with Kiribati, Samoa, and parts of Fiji and Tuvalu) is considered by the Seventh-day Adventist Church to be the same day as Saturday observed east of the IDL.[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39)

Most Seventh-day Adventists in [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa") planned to observe Sabbath on Sunday after Samoa's crossing the IDL in December 2011, but SDA groups in Samatau village and other places (approximately 300 members) decided to accept the IDL adjustment and observe the Sabbath on Saturday.[[37]](#cite_note-40) Debate continues within the Seventh-day Adventist community in the Pacific as to which day is really the seventh-day Sabbath.

The Samoan Independent Seventh-day Adventist Church, which is not affiliated to the worldwide Seventh-day Adventist Church, has decided to continue worshiping on Saturday, after a six-day week at the end of 2011.

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Islam")]

The [Islamic calendar](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar") and [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") communities recognize the convention of the IDL. In particular, the day for holding the *[Jumu'ah](/wiki/Jumu%27ah "Jumu'ah")* prayer appears to be local Friday everywhere in the world.[[d]](#cite_note-43) The IDL is not a factor in the start and end of Islamic lunar months. These depend solely on sighting the [new crescent moon](/wiki/Hilal_(crescent_moon) "Hilal (crescent moon)").[[40]](#cite_note-44) As an example, the fasts of the month of [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan") begin the morning after the crescent is sighted. That this day may vary in different parts of the world is well known in Islam. (see: [Ramadan § Beginning](/wiki/Ramadan#Beginning "Ramadan"))

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Judaism")]

Further information: [International date line in Judaism](/wiki/International_date_line_in_Judaism "International date line in Judaism")

The concept of an International Date Line in Jewish law is first mentioned by 12th-century decisors.[[41]](#cite_note-kashrus-45)[[42]](#cite_note-chabad-46) But it was not until the introduction of improved transportation and communications systems in the 20th century that the question of an International Date Line truly became a question of practical Jewish law.[[e]](#cite_note-47)

As a practical matter, the conventional International Date Line—or another line in the Pacific Ocean close to it—serves as a *de facto* date line for purposes of Jewish law, at least in existing Jewish communities. For example, residents of the Jewish communities of Japan,[[43]](#cite_note-48) New Zealand,[[44]](#cite_note-49) [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"),[[45]](#cite_note-50) and [French Polynesia](/wiki/French_Polynesia "French Polynesia")[[46]](#cite_note-51) all observe [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat") on local Saturday. However, there is not unanimity as to how Jewish law reaches that conclusion. For this reason, some authorities rule that certain aspects of Sabbath observance are required on Sunday (in Japan and New Zealand) or Friday (in Hawaii and French Polynesia) in addition to Saturday. Additionally, there are differences of opinion as to which day or days individual Jews traveling in the Pacific region away from established Jewish communities should observe Shabbat.[[41]](#cite_note-kashrus-45)[[42]](#cite_note-chabad-46)

For individuals crossing the IDL, the change of calendar date influences some aspects of practice under Jewish law. Yet other aspects depend on an individual's experience of sunsets and sunrises to count days, notwithstanding the calendar date.[[41]](#cite_note-kashrus-45)[[42]](#cite_note-chabad-46)

## Cultural references and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cultural references and traditions")]

### Line-crossing ceremonies relating to the IDL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Line-crossing ceremonies relating to the IDL")]

See also: [Line-crossing ceremony](/wiki/Line-crossing_ceremony "Line-crossing ceremony")

Ceremonies aboard ships to mark a sailor's or passenger's first crossing of the Equator, as well as crossing the International Date Line, have been long-held traditions in navies and in other maritime services around the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### In fiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: In fiction")]

* [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") utilized the concept to create a [paradox](/wiki/Paradox "Paradox") in his 1841 short story "Three Sundays in a Week", in which two navy captains circumnavigate the world in different directions and meet on a Sunday. One captain concludes that it is Saturday and the other concludes that it is Monday.[[47]](#cite_note-52)

* [Francis Bret Harte](/wiki/Bret_Harte "Bret Harte") referred to the concept in his 1867 poem "The Lost Galleon", about a Spanish galleon sailing between Acapulco and Manila in 1641 (a time when the *de facto* date line was west of the Philippines and Asia, making the poem historically inaccurate).[[48]](#cite_note-53)

* In [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne")'s novel *[Around the World in Eighty Days](/wiki/Around_the_World_in_Eighty_Days "Around the World in Eighty Days")* (1873), the main protagonist, [Phileas Fogg](/wiki/Phileas_Fogg "Phileas Fogg"), travels eastward around the world, betting with his friends that he could do it in 80 days. His servant [Jean Passepartout](/wiki/Jean_Passepartout "Jean Passepartout") keeps his watch set to London time during the journey. To win the wager, Fogg must return by 8:45 p.m. on Saturday, 21 December 1872. However, the journey suffers a series of delays and when Fogg reaches London, he believes it is 8:50 p.m. on Saturday, 21 December and that he has lost the wager by a margin of only five minutes. The next day, however, it is revealed that the day is Saturday, not Sunday, and Fogg arrives at his club just in time to win the bet. Verne explains that "while Phileas Fogg, going eastward, saw the sun pass the meridian eighty times, his friends in London only saw it pass the meridian seventy-nine times."[[49]](#cite_note-54)

* In [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco")'s novel *[The Island of the Day Before](/wiki/The_Island_of_the_Day_Before "The Island of the Day Before")* (1994), the protagonist finds himself on a becalmed ship, with an island close at hand on the other side of the IDL. Unable to swim, the protagonist indulges in increasingly imaginative speculation regarding the physical, metaphysical and religious importance of the IDL.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Date_Line&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** **Earliest time on Earth** here means that calendar day and clock time in this [time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") is *least* advanced (or the "*oldest* ") on Earth and that this place is the *last* (*latest* ) on Earth to reach any day and time. Because of that this time zone ([IDLW](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9212:00 "UTC−12:00")) is sometimes also called [Anywhere on Earth](/wiki/Anywhere_on_Earth "Anywhere on Earth") and deadlines can be set based on it e.g. "until the end of day Anywhere on Earth" means that if the day ended there it also ended everywhere else on the planet before that.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** **Latest time on Earth** here means that calendar day and clock time in this [time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") is *most* advanced (or the "*newest*") on Earth and that this place is the *first* (*earliest*) on Earth to reach any day and time.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Within the Papal Bull of Pope Gregory XIII that mandated the introduction of the Gregorian calendar in 1582. Italy, Portugal, and Spain (including its overseas possessions like the Philippines, and the Americas) skipped 10 days to make the process of a calendrical switch from the Julian to the Gregorian calendar. Thursday, October 4, was followed by Friday, October 15, 1582. Because the Julian calendar acknowledged a leap year day regularly and the Gregorian calendar did not during centennial years (ending with “00”, unless if the centennial year is evenly divisible to 400 years. Then the year will automatically be a "Century Leap Year"), the Julian calendar fell one day further behind, bringing the difference to 11 days from March 1 (O.S. February 19), 1700 until February 28 (O.S. February 17), 1800, and another one day to 12 days from March 1 (O.S. February 18), 1800 to February 28 (O.S. February 16), 1900; the count of excess days became considered as only 2 instead of 14 at the end of 1844, to acknowledge the 10-12 days that the Philippines lose during the period when it was using the American date.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** There has been some discussion that the [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") directly opposite [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), approximately 140°W, would be an appropriate date line for Islam. See Deen, S.M. ["Ramadan and Prayer Times in the Lands under Midnight Sun"](http://scienceunderislam.blogspot.com/2011/05/scid03-ramadan-and-prayer-times-in.html). *Science Digest for Muslim Thinkers*. Retrieved 18 May 2016. However, this appears to be an unusual, or at least only a theoretical, position. Muslims in areas that would be affected, such as Alaska[[38]](#cite_note-41) and Hawaii,[[39]](#cite_note-42) in fact hold *Jumu'ah* on local Friday, not local Thursday.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** The fleeing of [Jewish refugees](/wiki/Jewish_refugees "Jewish refugees"), including members of the [Mir Yeshiva](/wiki/Mir_Yeshiva_(Jerusalem)#Establishment_in_Jerusalem "Mir Yeshiva (Jerusalem)"), from the Nazis to China and Japan during World War II was a major impetus to the codification of this aspect of Jewish law. Many of the modern-era opinions cited in [[41]](#cite_note-kashrus-45) and [[42]](#cite_note-chabad-46) were produced in response to this situation.
